title: Core data model: Node, Justification, Nogood dataclasses shared across all modules
TMS engine: propagation, retraction cascades, restoration, BFS, truth computation
BFS cascade through dependents — the central algorithm
Truth computation: SL/CP with inlist AND and outlist negation
Dependency-directed backtracking to resolve contradictions
Dialectical argumentation via outlist mechanism
SQLite persistence: schema, save/load, FTS5 index, dependent rebuild
Functional API: _with_network pattern, namespace support, what-if simulation, dedup
Multi-agent federation: namespacing, active/inactive relay, sync semantics
LLM-driven derivation: prompt building, proposal parsing, exhaustive mode
Token-budgeted summary with priority ordering and summary node compression
Non-monotonic reasoning: how outlists enable "believe X unless Y", supersession, and challenges
How agents are namespaced, the active/inactive relay pattern, and kill-switch cascades
CLI entry point: 35+ subcommands, derive orchestration with async model invocation
How `source_hash` on Node is used to detect beliefs that may be outdated relative to their source files
Doyle's original Truth Maintenance System paper; understanding SL/CP justification semantics and the non-monotonic reasoning model this code implements
How nodes are pruned from the network while preserving semantic integrity (interacts with `summarize` and `recompute_all`)
Test cases for the challenge/defend cycle; the best way to understand the dialectical layering in practice
The truth evaluation function that `_propagate` calls at each step; understanding its semantics (especially the outlist / non-monotonic reasoning) is essential to predicting cascade behavior
An alternative to BFS propagation that iterates to a fixpoint over all nodes; compare when each strategy is appropriate
The primary caller that sets `_retracted` metadata before propagating; understanding the retract/assert lifecycle clarifies why `_propagate` skips retracted nodes
Test cases that exercise cascade and restoration scenarios, showing concrete examples of multi-hop propagation
The 1979 paper by Jon Doyle on Truth Maintenance Systems that this implementation follows; understanding SL justifications and dependency-directed backtracking gives the theoretical foundation
Key user of the outlist mechanism: makes old beliefs go OUT when replacements arrive
Doyle's 1979 paper on Support-List justifications and non-monotonic reasoning, the theoretical foundation for this code
The entrenchment-sorted backtracking logic that decides *which* premise to sacrifice; understanding the scoring in `_entrenchment` is key to predicting system behavior
Test cases that exercise the dependency-directed backtracking path, including edge cases around which premise gets chosen
Doyle's 1979 "A Truth Maintenance System" paper, specifically Section 4 on dependency-directed backtracking, which this code implements
The counterpart to challenge; uses `challenge` internally to attack the challenge node, creating the reversible dialectical cycle
The truth maintenance algorithm whose results Storage persists but never invokes
Round-trip tests that verify save/load preserves network state exactly
The `parse_beliefs` and `parse_nogoods` parsers that convert markdown to the intermediate dict format consumed by `_normalize_markdown`
Sync has complex edge cases (remote retraction, local re-assertion, `_retracted` flag lifecycle) — the tests document the expected behavior
The distinction between placing a node in antecedents (positive support) vs. outlist (defeater) is central to why the kill switch works correctly
Test cases that reveal edge cases in prompt building, parsing, and validation
Produces the `nodes` dict that `build_prompt` consumes; understanding its schema is essential
Creates summary nodes with `metadata["summarizes"]`, which is the mechanism compact uses for compression
Likely the full (non-budgeted) Markdown export; compare with compact to understand what gets lost during triage
The public API entry point that loads the network from SQLite and delegates to this function
Nogoods and OUT nodes bypass the budget, so compact output can exceed the specified limit; worth verifying whether callers account for this
How `find_culprits` scores premises by dependent count and entrenchment metadata to choose minimal-disruption retractions
Simulates a retraction without committing it, useful for understanding cascade blast radius before pulling the trigger
What happens when agent-a's beliefs depend on agent-b's beliefs and agent-b is killed; how `_resolve_namespace` enables these cross-agent references
How the derivation prompt is constructed from the belief network, including filtering, sampling, and budget constraints
The most complex function in this file; worth understanding the subprocess invocation, timeout handling, and exhaust-loop interaction
How `what_if_retract`/`what_if_assert` achieve read-only simulation while reporting cascading effects with depth information
The complementary function that backfills or updates `source_hash` on nodes; used after confirming a stale source change is expected
The path resolution logic and its `~/git/<name>` fallback convention, which is an implicit contract with the user's filesystem layout
How the CLI orchestrates check-stale → user review → retract/re-hash, and what happens to downstream derived beliefs when a source-backed premise is retracted
How agent namespaces (e.g., `agent-name:belief-id`) partition the belief space, with a single `ns:active` premise that can cascade-retract an entire agent's contributions
